entry.title: I'm learning Angular
entry.content:
Maybe using the Angular front end web development framework is like playing the factory building game Factorio?

One of the core building blocks of Angular, conceptually, or in relation to the architecture of Angular as a certain conceptual whole in itself, a component is a representation for a model, a view, and a controller unit. A component is Angular's implementation for there being a single model-view-controller unit.

Or something. I'm not sure. I don't know for certain. I have to do some more research to know for certain.

Anyway, you work within the framework of Angular and use all the building blocks, structures, and processes it gives you. You use all the information and methods available to you through the tools of the platform to produce products. All of them together forming one final product made from individual parts and systems.

As you leverage one of the framework's inversion of control features which is made possible with Angular's dependency injection system, you begin to encounter the difficulties associated with the complexity of designing and building large applications. Initial layouts or initially chosen design patterns end up being liabilities that must be restructured at later points in the timeline.

Each individual little factor that helps constitute the entire structure must now be refactored. Refactored into a better factor that supports a new form of the whole structure. Or refactoring for a whole new structure altogether. A much stronger structure.

Faster. Bigger. More powerful. It might do more stuff. And stuff is good. We like stuff. Stuff is what we want and being given more stuff from our production is definitely good.

Familiarity with TypeScript's syntax for object oriented programming is equivalent to understanding just one portion of the extensive and intimidating user interface. Beyond the TypeScript based user interface for coming up with object oriented classes, you also have to learn the hundreds of relationships that exist between individual concepts and throughout numerous areas. How to cleanly interact the idea of providers with the handling of function definitions, as one example.

Maybe there's thousands of things to understand? Or millions?

You start with basic abilities like creating basic directives, components, and modules and then graduate to advanced combinations and tiers. Maybe a challenging metagame emerges from the once crazy and impossible fray that you are now used to. Do not forget the need to program the behavior of multiple different lifecycle methods for individual components or directives.

And maybe directives themselves is just automation for component view handling?

Maybe there's some artificial intelligence and decision making associated with the directives in Angular? As they build HTML views for us, and with their building under our direction through a possibly Turing-complete language programming. This automation might be useful for greatly enhancing our production capabilities. Let us make the smart factory.

This is a complicated video game to play.

And this kind of stuff is fun and stimulating.

But I don't want just understanding of the Angular front end web development framework. No, I do not want only knowledge of how to use a tool and, particularly, in our case, a tool made for creating robust front end applications. Regardless of the amount of energy we have within us at the time, I do not want simply being able to solve a specific problem quickly through skill gained from extensive experience with an instrument and then call it a day, saying "We are finished!"

In the place of paltry expertise I want knowledge of the computer science fundamentals engine that powers a framework like Angular. I want to tackle Angular and also some algorithms and data structures stuff.

We need careful Turing-complete language navigation. And I'm not talking about the JavaScript language code you will type. Excellent module interface and decoupling planning is in store for us. And cost effective management of unwieldy states and state machines distributed throughout the application and even beyond the web browser. Just to name a few of the useful hidden secrets (or pitfalls!) associated with good software development.

I am motivated for the software freedom of big applications everywhere. This means that I believe that everyone should have the opportunity to have their own large scale business logic realized through software development. Without any costs. Without any restrictions imposed on the internal makeup of their software or on the possession of the software. No hidden required social contracts to adhere to or implied reciprocation to participate in. This very flexible freedom is for huge and complex software technology like the modern Angular web development platform.

My planned contribution to the world of free software which I plan on doing involves me having the goal of helping to further extend software development. For the sake of everyone. For the sake of anyone who would like to have in their possession the capability to make some very cool stuff. Production which may require countless hours of intelligence applied to diligent engineering and science. I want to contribute software and I want to share the powerful knowledge I want to gain.

So I have big and tough shoes to wear. But it is the journey and not the destination that should be your concern.

I think. I'm not sure. I have to research this too.
